category_summary:
Research breakthroughs led the conversation. **hardmaru** and **Burkov** spotlighted an ICLR 2026 paper [reframing residual connections](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-00b1b184f81e) as diffusion denoising, enabling independently trained network blocks and major memory savings.

- **Training & efficiency**: A top researcher's [thread on catastrophic forgetting](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-f9f0779ef2d8) during finetuning showed model size and optimization matter, with self-generated replay helping retention.
- **Infrastructure & performance**: **NVIDIA** drew strong engagement with HaoAI Lab's [video-gen speedup](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-6bcf6d0568c0) (25s to 4.2s) and **Dynamo Snapshot** [cutting Kubernetes cold-starts](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-0b7cc7978063) to under 5s. **Perplexity** [open-sourced a Unigram tokenizer](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-2d73b18f0dfc) reducing CPU use 5-6x, and **jerryjliu** [launched LiteParse v2](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-cc92c8321c0d), a Rust PDF parser.
- **Policy & ethics**: **Sam Altman** [announced a $250M](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-dcede8b60853) **OpenAI Foundation** commitment to broad prosperity. **Stanford HAI** [raised alarm with a 4M-application study](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-25dec8b783e7) finding racial bias in AI hiring tools.

A persistent undercurrent across many posts was **token economics**—organizations [blowing through budgets](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-6b5f2cd14f00) with unclear ROI, amplified by **Ethan Mollick**, **Gary Marcus**, and an [MIT-led paper](/?date=2026-05-28&category=social#item-b22cd52b0f8c) showing agents consume ~1000x more tokens than chat.

items.title: For over a decade, we’ve accepted that end-to-end backprop is the only way to train deep networks. B...
items.content:
For over a decade, we’ve accepted that end-to-end backprop is the only way to train deep networks. But holding the entire network in memory all at once is why AI training is hitting a resource wall.

We found a new way to break the network into blocks and train them independently. The trick? Treating the network’s forward pass like a diffusion model denoising a signal.

This reinterpretation slashes the memory needed to train deep models. In our #ICLR2026 paper (https://t.co/PK5h0mqQSo), we matched end-to-end performance across ViTs, DiTs, and LLMs. We did this while training just one isolated block at a time.

items.title: Training a deep neural network with standard backpropagation requires keeping every layer's intermed...
items.content:
Training a deep neural network with standard backpropagation requires keeping every layer's intermediate activations in memory until the backward pass uses them, so memory grows with depth and limits how large models can be made.

Previous attempts to split a network into pieces that train independently have been ad-hoc, performed noticeably worse than ordinary training, or only worked on classification tasks.

The authors of this ICLR 2026 paper notice that a residual connection (where a layer adds its output to its input instead of replacing it) is mathematically identical to one step of a diffusion model — a generative model trained by adding noise to data and learning to remove that noise step by step.

Since each noise level in a diffusion model can be trained on its own without depending on the others, the authors use this correspondence to partition a transformer into blocks, assign each block a range of noise levels, and train every block on its own denoising objective with no gradient flow between blocks.

Memory during training shrinks by a factor equal to the number of blocks, since only one block holds gradients at a time, and the same recipe works across vision transformers, image diffusion models, autoregressive language models, masked diffusion, and recurrent-depth models, matching ordinary end-to-end training in most cases and exceeding it in some.

Read with an AI tutor and quizzes for better retention:
https://t.co/INZYFz6YCU

PDF: https://t.co/AqKZe9RS32
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov explains the ICLR 2026 paper that equates residual connections to diffusion denoising steps, allowing transformers to be partitioned into independently trained blocks to cut memory use.
items.reasoning: Clear technical explainer of an important training-efficiency paper from a credible author, adding accessibility.
items.summary_html: Burkov explains the ICLR 2026 paper that equates residual connections to diffusion denoising steps, allowing transformers to be partitioned into independently trained blocks to cut memory use.

items.title:
New research reveals alarming patterns in AI hiring tools.

A large-scale study of 4 million job app...
items.content:
New research reveals alarming patterns in AI hiring tools.

A large-scale study of 4 million job applications found that 26% of Black applicants and 15% of Asian applicants faced algorithmic discrimination.

When one AI vendor screens for multiple employers, qualified candidates can be systematically rejected everywhere they apply.

As 90% of U.S. employers now use AI screening, understanding these tools' impact on the workforce becomes more urgent.

Hear more from the study's lead author @RishiBommasani or read our latest blog: https://t.co/NvIpUbYPQS

items.summary: Following yesterday's [Research](/?date=2026-05-27&category=research#item-108e5594fbc2) coverage of algorithmic monocultures in hiring, Stanford HAI shares research on AI hiring tools showing algorithmic discrimination against 26% of Black and 15% of Asian applicants across 4M applications, amplified when one vendor serves many employers.
items.reasoning: Credible institution, large-scale study, important AI-ethics/policy implications given 90% employer adoption; moderate engagement.

MIT just killed the idea that cheaper tokens mean cheaper AI.

A paper from MIT, Stanford, Google De...
items.content:
MIT just killed the idea that cheaper tokens mean cheaper AI.

A paper from MIT, Stanford, Google DeepMind, and Microsoft found agents use 1000x more tokens than chat.

The cost isn't the code they write. It's re-reading context on every loop.

Same task, same model: 30x cost variance.

items.title: Introducing Dynamo Snapshot, our approach for fast startup for inference workloads on Kubernetes, wh...
items.content:
Introducing Dynamo Snapshot, our approach for fast startup for inference workloads on Kubernetes, which reduces startup time from minutes to under 5 seconds.

In production inference deployments demand fluctuates over time. Cold-starting inference workloads can take minutes, leaving idle GPUs that generate no tokens and serve no requests.

Snapshot leverages GMS to enable concurrent weight restoration over a high-speed interconnect, while using Linux native AIO and parallel memfd restoration to accelerate CRIU restore performance.
items.author: @NVIDIAAI
items.summary: NVIDIA introduces Dynamo Snapshot, reducing Kubernetes inference cold-start from minutes to under 5 seconds using GMS, Linux AIO, and parallel CRIU restore.
items.reasoning: Concrete, technically detailed infrastructure advance from NVIDIA with solid engagement; relevant to production LLM serving.
items.summary_html: NVIDIA introduces Dynamo Snapshot, reducing Kubernetes inference cold-start from minutes to under 5 seconds using GMS, Linux AIO, and parallel CRIU restore.
items.content_html:
Introducing Dynamo Snapshot, our approach for fast startup for inference workloads on Kubernetes, which reduces startup time from minutes to under 5 seconds.

In production inference deployments demand fluctuates over time. Cold-starting inference workloads can take minutes, leaving idle GPUs that generate no tokens and serve no requests.

Snapshot leverages GMS to enable concurrent weight restoration over a high-speed interconnect, while using Linux native AIO and parallel memfd restoration to accelerate CRIU restore performance.

items.title: If we had done everything I suggested in my 2020 arXiv article “The Next Decade in AI”, we might act...
items.content:
If we had done everything I suggested in my 2020 arXiv article “The Next Decade in AI”, we might actually have reached AGI by now.

In the last three years, after a detour driven by the false promise of pure scaling, we have gone a long way to the first of the four goals I laid out: neurosymbolic AI. (Claude Code; DeepMind’s new math system that solved 9 Erdos problems, etc). Harnesses and tools, for example.

But the field has made little progress on the other three:
- databases of explicit, systematic, machine interpretable knowledge [LLMs are a partial but inadequate/unreliably substitute]
- reliable reasoning systems that can work with partial and incomplete information
- the ability to represent and induce explicit world models (which I called cognitive models in 2020) that AI systems can reason over.

An excess commitment to pure scaling delayed all of these.

If we focus on the other three AGI may be achievable, possibly in the next decade.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus revisits his 2020 essay The Next Decade in AI, arguing neurosymbolic progress has occurred via Claude Code and DeepMind math systems, but three other AGI goals remain unmet due to overcommitment to scaling.
items.reasoning: Substantive, original argument from a prominent researcher about AGI roadmaps and neurosymbolic AI, though modest engagement.

cognition is now the largest independent agent lab in the world. take the 200% utilization that everyone is hitting  from this chart and run out the sales growth from this, i encourage you to go thru the exercise if you are new to investing

a lot of you have read my cog initation post, but when i talk to people they do not sufficiently appreciate that this is the perfect storm of all the trends:

- first* koding agent
- first* cloud dev infra
- best reviewed code review/security guy
- first* llm wiki knowledge base
- s-tier GTM
- most IOI golds
- somehow also cracked at Smash Bros and Poker

agent lab gets you:
- long model diversity
- long reasoning/toolcalling models
- long harnesses
- long domain specific RLFT
- long coding data/expertise/evals
- long full agentic SDLC
- chief partner to CIOs in combating tokenmaxxing slop from humans and from agents
- SOTA foodbench from house chefs

and this is why you can see this is Peter Thiel's biggest AI bet

*i dont like "first" as an adjective, but "first + 2 years of serious scaling" really serves as a shorthand for "most enterprise battle tested" - see customer list in screenshot - literally 10s of thousands of devs, 10s of thousands of repos, PER CUSTOMER is the kind of weight I put on the simple word "first"

items.title: The most likely way continual learning manifests in the coming few years is through products used di...
items.content:
The most likely way continual learning manifests in the coming few years is through products used directly for knowledge work. 

Sort of how cursor can continually train their models with real-world data and RL, Claude, Copilot, and co will see if they can for knowledge work.

I was chatting with Ronak a few weeks ago when this was crystalizing for me, so it's fun to see a startup in that area.

items.title:
I'm going extremely hard on sub-agents with Claude Code.

Everything that can be an agent, should be...
items.content:
I'm going extremely hard on sub-agents with Claude Code.

Everything that can be an agent, should be an agent.

I mean, not really, but that's the best way I have to learn.

Eventually, I'll know enough to find a better balance, but for now, sub-agents everywhere, every time.

Anthropic is also going hard on TUI support for agents, which is awesome!

items.title: When they write fiction, AIs are obsessed by things that take or give memories, contracts with senti...
items.content:
When they write fiction, AIs are obsessed by things that take or give memories, contracts with sentient inanimate objects, sets of secretive rules that govern conduct & which no one can acknowledge out loud... 

All very on-the-nose. I suspect a lot is hyperstition at this point.

items.title: At production input lengths, the encoder cuts p50 latency by roughly 5× vs. HuggingFace tokenizers, ...
items.content:
At production input lengths, the encoder cuts p50 latency by roughly 5× vs. HuggingFace tokenizers, 2× vs. SentencePiece C++, and 1.5× vs. IREE C.

At 514 tokens, it runs in 63 µs with zero heap allocations. https://t.co/PBg08lAXc8

items.title:
Building products is all about tradeoffs.

Seeing this, I see the tradeoffs being made.

Wanting to ...
items.content:
Building products is all about tradeoffs.

Seeing this, I see the tradeoffs being made.

Wanting to keep the price low means not using those fancy hands.

Wanting to keep power consumption low means not using legs.

Wanting to get something out there that's not yet generalized means building robots that do specific tasks.

It is what we got, will it find a customer?

At $10 an hour maybe.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Analyzes product tradeoffs in low-cost robots: skipping advanced hands and legs to control price and power, building task-specific instead of generalized robots, and questions market fit at low hourly cost.
items.reasoning: Substantive product-strategy analysis of robotics commercialization from a known tech commentator with moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Analyzes product tradeoffs in low-cost robots: skipping advanced hands and legs to control price and power, building task-specific instead of generalized robots, and questions market fit at low hourly cost.
items.content_html:
Building products is all about tradeoffs.

Seeing this, I see the tradeoffs being made.

Wanting to keep the price low means not using those fancy hands.

Wanting to keep power consumption low means not using legs.

Wanting to get something out there that's not yet generalized means building robots that do specific tasks.

It is what we got, will it find a customer?

At $10 an hour maybe.

items.title:
You won't run Claude Code the same way after this. 

AI coding sessions often fall apart the same wa...
items.content:
You won't run Claude Code the same way after this. 

AI coding sessions often fall apart the same way. 

You ask for a feature, nine files get edited, something silently breaks.
 
Claude Code Harness wraps the model in a real delivery loop.
 
It forces a strict cycle before any code ships:
 
1. Plan the work
2. Execute in parallel
3. Review from four angles
4. Release with a changelog
 
The review layer checks security, performance, code quality, and accessibility.
 
Nothing reaches your repo without passing through it.
 
The new v4 release rebuilds the entire engine in Go.
 
Every tool call used to add 40-60ms of overhead. 

That dropped under 10ms, making sessions feel instantly responsive.
 
Node.js is no longer required, and startup dropped to 30ms.
 
It is fully open source and installs as a single plugin.
 
Could this become the default operating system for AI engineers?

You won't run Claude Code the same way after this.

AI coding sessions often fall apart the same way.

You ask for a feature, nine files get edited, something silently breaks.

Claude Code Harness wraps the model in a real delivery loop.

It forces a strict cycle before any code ships:

1. Plan the work

2. Execute in parallel

3. Review from four angles

4. Release with a changelog

The review layer checks security, performance, code quality, and accessibility.

Nothing reaches your repo without passing through it.

The new v4 release rebuilds the entire engine in Go.

Every tool call used to add 40-60ms of overhead.

That dropped under 10ms, making sessions feel instantly responsive.

Node.js is no longer required, and startup dropped to 30ms.

It is fully open source and installs as a single plugin.

Could this become the default operating system for AI engineers?

items.title:
One command just turned Claude Code into 100 agents that learn. 

Claude Code is powerful, but it ru...
items.content:
One command just turned Claude Code into 100 agents that learn. 

Claude Code is powerful, but it runs as a single agent. 

No shared memory, no parallel delegation, no cross-task coordination.
 
Ruflo, formerly claude-flow, fixes that with one command.
 
A single npx init spins up 100 specialized agents in your terminal. 

They handle coding, testing, security audits, docs, and architecture in parallel.
 
Here is what makes it work:
 
> Self-learning memory across sessions
> Vector search 12,500x faster
> Zero-trust federation across machines
> Background workers triggered on commits
 
Agents on different boxes can discover each other and authenticate over mTLS. 

Sensitive data gets stripped before anything leaves your node.
 
It also plugs into GPT, Gemini, and Ollama with smart routing.
 
Plain-English goals get compiled into executable plans through a GOAP planner.
 
The whole repo is open-source. So what would you delegate first?

items.title:
New trend: continual learning.

I first showed you this with Levangie Labs from @blevlabs. 

You "gr...
items.content:
New trend: continual learning.

I first showed you this with Levangie Labs from @blevlabs. 

You "grow" an agent by teaching it. It gets better over time.

It works because it has a better memory, so can try things, and learn, and remembers what it learned, so it gets better over time.

Which makes it hard to get. Starts out like a brilliant 16 year old, then you gotta take it to university. 

I spent some time last week with Ronak learning about his tech and company. Quite excellent, which explains why he got funded.

I'm not compensated by Ronak, just trying to feature great new AI startups.

items.title:
Agentic orchestration is now the difference between junior and senior developers.

Every cracked dev...
items.content:
Agentic orchestration is now the difference between junior and senior developers.

Every cracked developer I know is running multiple agents to build their stuff.

I've tried and failed several times. Impossible to keep too much context in my head.

I spent the weekend testing LobeHub on a backlog of issues. I had about 25 open issues on one of my GitHub projects.

I now have 0.

LobeHub's new Chief Agent Operator can dispatch parallel Claude Code sessions across all of your GitHub issues.

Each session works in a separate worktree and retries on any failure.

At the end, you get a PR for each solved issue. Your job becomes reviewing them.

This whole thing acts like a new layer on top of the agents you are already running. Without this orchestration layer, there's no way you can keep multiple sessions going on.

Here is the link to try this out: https://t.co/lC8DA1Wi35

#paid partnership.

items.content_html:
The Future of AI Hyperlocal Intelligence

He built McDonalds' first AI and we talk about changes in retail and enterprises due to AI.

Michael Koch is a 4x Exited Tech Founder, CEO of HubKonnect, and creator of Hyperlocal Intelligence, a new category within Enterprise AI focused on real-world, localized physical intelligence and continuous learning and decision-making at scale.

Learn more about his company at https://t.co/uqmnAckrci

items.title: Someone just asked me “What I don’t get is why you see it as some kind of failure that AI labs are n...
items.content:
Someone just asked me “What I don’t get is why you see it as some kind of failure that AI labs are now using harnesses and neurosymbolic tools…?”

I don’t! As I argued in my newsletter on Claude Code (entitled “The biggest advance in AI since the LLM”) and in earlier piece o3 and grok 4,  I see this trend as absolute vindication for what I have been arguing for the last 30 years - that neurosymbolic tools have to be part of the solution.

People will soon forget that they ever argued otherwise, but they did, right here, for a decade. 🤷‍♂️

items.title: I have agents working independently on an app, and I also work myself, so it often happens that I as...
items.content:
I have agents working independently on an app, and I also work myself, so it often happens that I ask Codex to "pull the changes from Git by preserving the local changes, then merge the two, and push to Git."

This is not 100% safe because Codex can mess up and lose local changes.

I think there should be a button for this.

items.title:
❓How do Data Engineering investments shape AI and BI outcomes?

╰┈➤Find out in the 2026 Dresner Advi...
items.content:
❓How do Data Engineering investments shape AI and BI outcomes?

╰┈➤Find out in the 2026 Dresner Advisory Services Data Engineering Market Study Report, made available by @Informatica here: https://t.co/ffi6TYp6TC 

💥”Slowly, then all at once.💥 Data warehousing, replication, data discovery/query, and analytics have been persistent and systematically growing data engineering use cases over many years, but the report reveals what has happened recently. In 2025, several new use cases became critically important to enterprises, and then suddenly in 2026 all use cases significantly rose in critical importance. These include activities that involve data flows, data delivery, data migration, and extraction to external systems — AI must carry the primary responsibility for these shifts, as delivering data to AI applications in many different operational settings is the new normal. See the upper half of the attached infographic.

🌐”An abundance of riches.”🌐 The vendor ratings are also revealing. As shown in the lower half of the infographic, @Informatica is tied for 1st place in the rankings, which are based on development and usability features, processing and transformation of data, and data sources and targets. But it doesn't stop there. The 92-page PDF report presents deep details on a much wider spectrum of metrics and industry benchmarks. This includes many dozens (hundreds?) of dimensions of data engineering capabilities, services, deployments, customer adoption, innovation, future plans, and those ubiquitous use cases.

𝗤𝘂𝗶𝗰𝗸 𝗦𝘂𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆:
🔶In 2026, 48% of organizations rate data engineering as critical — nearly double the 28% who said "critical" in 2019 — reflecting a fundamental change in how organizations are building for AI and analytics performance.
🔶AI is driving that change across hundreds of organizations globally. Among organizations where AI is a cornerstone of their business strategy, 88% rate data engineering as critical or very important.
🔶What matters now is whether your organization is keeping pace with these developments.

𝗡𝗲𝘅𝘁 𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽𝘀: IT and data leaders who want to keep up should download this study to learn this:
💠Why the highest-ROI business intelligence organizations invest in data engineering differently than their peers.
💠Which capabilities and sourcing models define leaders in 2026.
💠Where your adoption stands relative to industry benchmarks.

╰┈➤ 𝗗𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗹𝗼𝗮𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁 𝘁𝗼𝗱𝗮𝘆: https://t.co/ffi6TYp6TC 

#sponsored

items.title:
The pitch for one of San Francisco's startup factories, er, residencies. 

I found Brayden last year...
items.content:
The pitch for one of San Francisco's startup factories, er, residencies. 

I found Brayden last year here on X and gave you first look at him. 

He tells me that being in the Residency was a pressure cooker. They took care of everything else in his life so he could focus on just building his company. 

Next to his desk was a company building space-based datacenters. And another building security systems (even hacked him and found some things he needed to fix) and about seven others. 

At the end of these things he pitched a room full of investors and has been busy with fundraising ever since (that was a few days ago).

Thanks @blevlabs for being the first to show me continual learning AI's that built https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ for me. Which reads 30,000 posts a day here on X from the AI community and catches me up on things I missed.

items.title: At IEEE ISCAS in Shanghai, Huawei's He Tingbo introduced the Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers...
items.content:
At IEEE ISCAS in Shanghai, Huawei's He Tingbo introduced the Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her's Law.

It's a post-Moore path focused on one thing:

Reducing time across devices, circuits, chips, and systems.

For AI inference, time is the cost driver. Tau Scaling Law attacks it at the system level.

items.summary: Describes Huawei's He Tingbo introducing the Tau Scaling Law at IEEE ISCAS as a post-Moore approach focused on reducing time across devices and systems.

items.title: I don't know anyone who quit their job to live of savings and built something that made money before...
items.content:
I don't know anyone who quit their job to live of savings and built something that made money before their savings run out except @AndreyAzimov

I always think it's the wrong way to do it

I think you should build something on the side and once it makes equal or more money than your main job or freelance gigs and it's stable, quit the job and switch

Being unemployed somehow makes people lazy and feel too relaxed to build something, like your day is fully free of commitments which sounds ideal but freedom isn't when stuff is built I think, you need constraints

I had income from my YouTube channel Panda Mix Show in 2013 when I started, and it took over a year before I made enough money to switch

Obviously I wish him well but I think better do the switch than hard quit

items.title:
@RobGsix Really rich people need more than a driver: security. 

The reason they will be driven arou...
items.content:
@RobGsix Really rich people need more than a driver: security. 

The reason they will be driven around is because of security. 

The President won't ever take an autonomous vehicle around for that reason. 

Zuckerberg said he learned to fly a helicopter to have freedom from the security concerns. 

It's why I don't like calling everything for rich people "luxury." 

Luxury to me is having a nice vacation house on a beach, or something like that. 

This goes to concerns the hyper rich have.

Been talking to a lot of Chinese about this. They are big users of having people driving them around. They need the flexibility to have people picked up at the airport, or a car waiting at their front door when they need to go somewhere. Autonomy isn't yet good enough for that. 

In other words, I agree. Autonomy won't be appropriate for many of the world's hyper rich. At least not for years.

items.title:
Every AI response depends on more than compute.

It also depends on:

Memory access speed

Interconn...
items.content:
Every AI response depends on more than compute.

It also depends on:

Memory access speed

Interconnect efficiency

Chip-to-chip communication overhead

Data movement costs

End-to-end system latency

When any of these slow down, your AI gets slower and more expensive.

The model isn't the bottleneck. The system is.

It's no longer just: which model are we running?

Infrastructure-level thinking is becoming the real AI differentiator.

items.title: "The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book" by Andriy Burkov is an excellent and highly valuable resour...
items.content:
"The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book" by Andriy Burkov is an excellent and highly valuable resource. It has garnered widespread praise from both industry leaders and everyday readers for successfully condensing a vast field into a remarkably accessible format. 

Its strengths are substantial, yet it's best used as a strategic first step rather than a comprehensive, final authority. Here’s a detailed breakdown of whether it’s a good choice for you.

*   Unmatched Clarity and Brevity: The book's core value is its ability to offer a coherent and comprehensive overview without the fluff. Reviewers consistently praise the author for striking an excellent balance between concise explanations and technical rigor, making complex ideas click into place naturally.
*   Breadth of Coverage in Minimal Space: In roughly 140 pages, it covers a wide array of essential machine learning concepts. This includes supervised and unsupervised learning, neural networks, deep learning, ensemble methods, and practical topics like feature engineering and model evaluation.
*   Exceptional for Refreshers and First-Pass Learning: The book is frequently recommended as a "refresher" for experienced practitioners who need a quick mental map of the field, and as a "first pass" for newcomers to build a conceptual framework before diving into denser textbooks. It provides the "why" and "what" to complement the "how" found in more code-focused resources.
*   Strong Endorsements from Industry Leaders: The book has received glowing praise from prominent figures, including:
    *   **Peter Norvig**: Research Director at Google, who called it a "solid introduction to the field".
    *   **Aurélien Géron**: Author of the bestseller "Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow".
    *   **Gareth James**: Co-author of "An Introduction to Statistical Learning" (ISL).
    *   **Leadership from Amazon, LinkedIn, and eBay**: Who have all praised its practical, time-efficient value.

The book is for you if you are:
    *   A software engineer, scientist, or student seeking a fast, structured, and efficient introduction to the machine learning landscape.
    *   An experienced practitioner wanting a high-level refresher or a compact reference guide.
    *   A manager or curious professional aiming for confidence when discussing AI concepts with technical teams.

#LMtrainingData

items.content:
Is your billing process costing you more than just time?

As a business owner, every hour lost to billing chaos is revenue left on the table.

I hired help last year. Still spent hours briefing, chasing, double-checking.

These bottlenecks are only getting worse.

As AI and agentic commerce change how companies monetize, legacy systems simply can’t support complex, real-time usage models.

Billing shouldn't slow your growth. It should fuel it.
@airwallex just launched Billing, one platform for invoicing, subscriptions, usage metering, payments & reconciliation.

More time closing deals. Less time fixing tools.

items.content_html:
@jewiss @emkwan I did a lot of consumer research for the auto industry. Consumers never tell you what they want in the future; you have to show it to them. Or you have to at least paint a picture of what they would get if you built something.

This is what people don't understand and why they miss what the future looks like. Thanks for watching!

This is why Tesla took so much away from the others.

items.title:
@emkwan The problem is it looks exactly like that: that it was forced into doing this. 

It doesn't ...
items.content:
@emkwan The problem is it looks exactly like that: that it was forced into doing this. 

It doesn't go far enough. 

Its screens aren't big enough.

It doesn't push technology far enough. 

Doesn't look like it's an amazing driving machine. 

Doesn't push ahead of the Chinese in innovation. 

Just looks like a project trying to move Ferrari into the modern age. 

Which pisses off its radical fan base that looks to Ferrari for driving experience. 

More than one of its customers has told me that they bought Ferrari BECAUSE it takes them back to the past, when cars were fast and looked that way and when the purr of the engine made them feel like a million bucks.

When I've driven one it starts making love to you at about 120 mph. 

This doesn't look like it would do that. And the sound of the engine is part of it. 

But worse, it doesn't make a statement like other Ferraris do on the freeway. Which says "I'm successful and I'm driving a car that reminds me of that." 

This looks like a Hyundai. Too many round shapes. Too feminine. 

If Ferrari's full force was behind it this would have made a statement about the future. Instead it just says to me "we are behind the Chinese."
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Detailed critique that Ferrari's EV looks forced and fails to push technology or innovation ahead of Chinese rivals, alienating its driving-focused fan base.
items.reasoning: Highest engagement in batch with a substantive argument, but off AI/ML core and primarily about automotive branding.
items.summary_html: Detailed critique that Ferrari's EV looks forced and fails to push technology or innovation ahead of Chinese rivals, alienating its driving-focused fan base.
items.content_html:
@emkwan The problem is it looks exactly like that: that it was forced into doing this.

It doesn't go far enough.

Its screens aren't big enough.

It doesn't push technology far enough.

Doesn't look like it's an amazing driving machine.

Doesn't push ahead of the Chinese in innovation.

Just looks like a project trying to move Ferrari into the modern age.

Which pisses off its radical fan base that looks to Ferrari for driving experience.

More than one of its customers has told me that they bought Ferrari BECAUSE it takes them back to the past, when cars were fast and looked that way and when the purr of the engine made them feel like a million bucks.

When I've driven one it starts making love to you at about 120 mph.

This doesn't look like it would do that. And the sound of the engine is part of it.

But worse, it doesn't make a statement like other Ferraris do on the freeway. Which says "I'm successful and I'm driving a car that reminds me of that."

This looks like a Hyundai. Too many round shapes. Too feminine.

If Ferrari's full force was behind it this would have made a statement about the future. Instead it just says to me "we are behind the Chinese."

The takeaway for me:

Cable-free broadcast is not just a media story.

It is an enterprise operations story.

When connectivity is flexible, teams can rethink where work happens, how fast content moves, and what “live” can actually mean.

Learn more: https://t.co/8l1WLRTZ5A

What workflow in your business is still being limited by fixed infrastructure?

T-Mobile for Business Partner

The cameras can already move.

The most interesting example was the mobile 4D capture system.

It enables:

→ 360 degree swing anal...
items.content:
The most interesting example was the mobile 4D capture system.

It enables:

→ 360 degree swing analysis
→ Real-time broadcast-quality content delivery
→ Cameras that follow the talent, not the other way around

That last point is the shift.

Production starts to become mobile by default.

items.title:
Nah the definitions of left and right really don't work anymore I think

We're in a completely diffe...
items.content:
Nah the definitions of left and right really don't work anymore I think

We're in a completely different world

The liberal left of the 1960s hippies would now be classified as extreme right:
- anti pharma and vaccines
- anti war
- distrust of the CIA (and deep state)
- anti mainstream media
- off grid, self sufficient, trad life
- anti processed food, anti GMO, anti seed oils, instead pro organic/bio food
- free speech absolutism
- anti psychiatry and psych meds
- use psychedelics instead of meds

So I don't think the terms make sense anymore
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: levelsio posting a political essay on how left/right labels no longer apply, listing once-leftist positions now coded as right.
items.reasoning: High engagement but off-topic political commentary with no AI relevance.
items.summary_html: levelsio posting a political essay on how left/right labels no longer apply, listing once-leftist positions now coded as right.
items.content_html:
Nah the definitions of left and right really don't work anymore I think

We're in a completely different world

The liberal left of the 1960s hippies would now be classified as extreme right:

  • anti pharma and vaccines
  • anti war
  • distrust of the CIA (and deep state)
  • anti mainstream media
  • off grid, self sufficient, trad life
  • anti processed food, anti GMO, anti seed oils, instead pro organic/bio food
  • free speech absolutism
  • anti psychiatry and psych meds
  • use psychedelics instead of meds

So I don't think the terms make sense anymore

items.content:
Then it moves beyond the range.

Out on the course, aerial fly cams can go tee to green.

Photographers can upload via 5G Photojournalism.

Production teams can stay connected through network-based production layers, in collaboration with Clear-Com.

Different workflows.

Same underlying idea:

The network becomes the production layer.

items.title:
Live production has always been shaped by infrastructure.

Where can the cameras go?

Where can the ...
items.content:
Live production has always been shaped by infrastructure.

Where can the cameras go?

Where can the crew connect?

How fast can footage move from capture to distribution?

At the Range Show, 5G On Demand changes that equation:

→ No fixed infrastructure
→ No visible cables
→ Broadcast-quality delivery from wherever the action is
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Discusses how 5G On Demand removes fixed infrastructure constraints for live production at a range show.

items.title:
@sergey_nog @Ferrari Last year I met someone who was driving a $30 million car (a 1959 Jaguar). 

As...
items.content:
@sergey_nog @Ferrari Last year I met someone who was driving a $30 million car (a 1959 Jaguar). 

Asked him: "why do you collect cars like this?"

"I want a time machine that takes me back to the past."

This car tries to take people into the future, but fails at that. Its screens aren't big enough, for instance, and it doesn't have any futuristic technology like a Tesla has.

So it is stuck in between the two customer groups, which a brand like Ferrari never should be. And it pisses off people who see Ferrari as a driving machine with tech from the future. (Ferrari owners tell me they hate electric vehicles). 

You are right.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Robert Scoble discusses Ferrari being stuck between heritage and futuristic tech, contrasting it with Tesla, in a reply about car collecting and EVs.
items.reasoning: Moderate engagement but off-topic from AI/ML, focused on automotive branding.
items.summary_html: Robert Scoble discusses Ferrari being stuck between heritage and futuristic tech, contrasting it with Tesla, in a reply about car collecting and EVs.

"I want a time machine that takes me back to the past."

This car tries to take people into the future, but fails at that. Its screens aren't big enough, for instance, and it doesn't have any futuristic technology like a Tesla has.

So it is stuck in between the two customer groups, which a brand like Ferrari never should be. And it pisses off people who see Ferrari as a driving machine with tech from the future. (Ferrari owners tell me they hate electric vehicles).

"That's the minimum of our hotel sir"

So then I thought let's open the window, but 

"The windows are locked for security reasons sir"

Another year, still degrowth

But Ferrari is about driving experience.

Its launch will be studied for decades for what not to do.

items.title:
So I don't know if this is a thing in other countries (probably?)

But Dutch do a thing called dropp...
items.content:
So I don't know if this is a thing in other countries (probably?)

But Dutch do a thing called dropping, if you're like 10 to 15 years old your parents drop you with friends in a forest with no phone, but maybe a compass and map and you have to find your way back to a city

items.title: @typesfast No, it's weirdly safe from my anecdotal experience as a Dutch person living in Netherland...
items.content:
@typesfast No, it's weirdly safe from my anecdotal experience as a Dutch person living in Netherlands the first 27 years of my life

Sometimes you fall, usually cause of frozen water on road (ice) and you slip, but you just get a bruise that's it

You pretty much never hit your head, I don't know why

The biggest danger ofc is cars but many roads have separated bicycle lanes

items.content: @Nale When I was in my teens I really got into track and field (high jump). Traveled with my team around for a few competitions - but my parents never went to any of my events or even competitions! No one expected such treatment.
